results.possibleIdentity: Barbara Gormley was a poet and administrative leader in the New York avant-garde scene during the 1960s and 1970s. She is historically significant for her role as the Managing Director of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in the late 1960s, specifically appearing in that capacity in the organization's 1968 newsletters and administrative records. In addition to her administrative work facilitating collaborations between artists and engineers, she was a contributing poet to the 'Eventorium Muse' (1964–1967), a literary and art journal associated with the Eventorium group on Manhattan's Upper West Side. This group, which included figures like Michael Benedikt and Frank Kuenstler, was known for its interest in surrealism and prose poetry.
sources: https://www.cdlib.org/ (Experiments in Art and Technology Records - Online Archive of California)

results.possibleIdentity: William Soghor is an artist and entrepreneur active in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known for his participation in the significant 1973 group exhibition 'Soft as Art' at the New York Cultural Center, which explored the use of soft materials and fibers in contemporary sculpture. In this exhibition, his work was shown alongside major figures of the era, including Robert Rauschenberg, Claes Oldenburg, Richard Serra, and Robert Mapplethorpe. Soghor is also associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, appearing in archival references and art book indexes alongside the organization. Beyond his personal artistic practice, he founded the William Soghor Company, which provided specialized professional supplies for the art world, notably designing artwork inventory systems and file cards with integrated windows for 35mm slides. He is a long-time resident of Manhattan and is married to the painter Phyllis Herfield.

results.possibleIdentity: Agnes Denes (born May 31, 1931, in Budapest, Hungary) is a pioneering Hungarian-born American conceptual artist known for her work in Land Art, environmental art, and ecological art. She became active in the New York art scene in the 1960s and 1970s, establishing herself through a unique approach she calls "Visual Philosophy," which integrates science, mathematics, philosophy, and linguistics into her artistic practice. In the late 1960s, she joined the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) collective, an organization founded to facilitate collaborations between artists and engineers. Her involvement is documented in the E.A.T. records from 1969 and 1970, and she submitted ambitious project proposals to the organization during its "Projects Outside Art" call. Denes was also one of the few women included in the landmark 1970 exhibition "Software: Information Technology: Its New Meaning for Art" at the Jewish Museum, where she collaborated with the computer scientist group R.E.S.I.S.T.O.R.S. and utilized data from AT&T Bell Laboratories for her "Philosophical Drawings." Her notable early works include the site-specific performance "Rice/Tree/Burial" (1968), and she later achieved widespread recognition for "Wheatfield — A Confrontation" (1982), where she planted and harvested a two-acre field of wheat on a landfill in Manhattan near the World Trade Center.
